annotations.label: Probability theory

anchor.snippet: is the branch of mathematics concerned with probability

annotations.note: A description of a mathematical field, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Event

anchor.snippet: Any specified subset of the sample space is called an event

annotations.note: Events are formalized as measurable subsets of the sample space via MeasurableSet.

annotations.label: Sample space

anchor.snippet: is called the sample space of the experiment

annotations.note: The sample space is represented as the underlying type equipped with a MeasurableSpace, with no dedicated 'sample space' definition.

annotations.label: Probability

anchor.snippet: Probability is a way of assigning every

annotations.note: Assigning each event a value in [0,1] with total mass 1 is exactly IsProbabilityMeasure (μ univ = 1).

annotations.label: Probability distribution

anchor.snippet: To qualify as a probability distribution

annotations.note: The total-mass-one normalization requirement is captured by the bundled ProbabilityMeasure type.

annotations.label: Probability of {1,6},{3},{2,4}

anchor.snippet: will occur is 5/6

annotations.note: A specific numerical worked example with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Random variable

anchor.snippet: is a function that assigns to each elementary event in the sample space a real number

annotations.note: Random variables are formalized as measurable functions (Measurable/AEMeasurable), with no standalone 'random variable' definition.

annotations.label: Coin flip random variable

anchor.snippet: when flipping a coin the two possible outcomes are

annotations.note: An illustrative coin-flip example, not a formalized statement.

annotations.label: Discrete probability theory

anchor.snippet: deals with events that occur in countable sample spaces

annotations.note: Discrete probability over countable spaces is supported via PMF, though the sub-field description is not itself a statement.

annotations.label: Discrete experiments

anchor.snippet: Throwing dice , experiments with decks of cards

annotations.note: A list of motivating examples with no corresponding declaration.

annotations.label: Classical definition of probability

anchor.snippet: the number of cases favorable for the event

annotations.note: The favorable/total-cases ratio is the conditioned counting measure uniformOn, satisfying uniformOn s t = #(s∩t)/#s.

annotations.label: Even number on a die

anchor.snippet: occurrence of an even number when a dice is rolled

annotations.note: A worked example, not a formalized result.

annotations.label: Modern definition (discrete)

anchor.snippet: The modern definition starts with a finite or countable set called the sample space

annotations.note: A function on a countable sample space valued in [0,1] summing to 1 is exactly PMF.

annotations.label: Probability of an event

anchor.snippet: The probability of the event

annotations.note: P(E) = Σ_{ω∈E} p(ω) is PMF.toMeasure_apply (tsum of the indicator of the pmf).

annotations.label: Probability mass function

anchor.snippet: is called a probability mass function abbreviated as pmf

annotations.note: The probability mass function is exactly the PMF type.

annotations.label: Continuous probability theory

anchor.snippet: deals with events that occur in a continuous sample space

annotations.note: Continuous distributions are formalized via densities (HasPDF), but the sub-field description is not itself a statement.

annotations.label: Cumulative distribution function

anchor.snippet: a function called the cumulative distribution function

annotations.note: The CDF is ProbabilityTheory.cdf, satisfying cdf μ x = μ.real (Iic x).

annotations.label: Properties of the CDF

anchor.snippet: The CDF necessarily satisfies the following properties

annotations.note: Monotonicity (monotone_cdf), limits 0 at -∞ and 1 at +∞ (tendsto_cdf_atBot/atTop), and right-continuity (from StieltjesFunction) are all proved.

annotations.label: Continuous probability distribution

anchor.snippet: is said to have a continuous probability distribution if the corresponding CDF

annotations.note: Continuity of the CDF corresponds to an atomless (NoAtoms) measure; there is no dedicated 'continuous distribution' predicate.

annotations.label: Probability density function

anchor.snippet: is said to have a probability density function

annotations.note: The probability density function is MeasureTheory.pdf, with existence captured by the HasPDF class.

annotations.label: CDF exists for all random variables

anchor.snippet: the CDF exists for all random variables

annotations.note: The cdf definition is total—defined for every (probability) measure on ℝ regardless of discrete/continuous nature.

annotations.label: Mixed discrete-continuous distribution

anchor.snippet: a mix of discrete and continuous distributions

annotations.note: An illustrative example of distributions that are neither purely discrete nor continuous, with no dedicated declaration.

annotations.label: Cantor distribution

anchor.snippet: the Cantor distribution has no positive probability for any single point

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Cantor set/space in topology but not the Cantor probability distribution.

annotations.label: Probability measure

anchor.snippet: is called a probability measure if

annotations.note: A countably additive measure with μ univ = 1 is exactly IsProbabilityMeasure (additivity from Measure).

annotations.label: Unique measure for each CDF

anchor.snippet: then there is a unique probability measure on

annotations.note: Existence via StieltjesFunction.measure with round-trip cdf_measure_stieltjesFunction, and uniqueness via Measure.eq_of_cdf, establish the CDF↔measure correspondence.

annotations.label: Probability of a set

anchor.snippet: The probability of a set

annotations.note: The probability of a measurable set is the probability measure applied to it (μ s).

annotations.label: Radon–Nikodym density

anchor.snippet: the Radon–Nikodym theorem is used to define a density as the Radon–Nikodym derivative

annotations.note: The density as a Radon–Nikodym derivative is Measure.rnDeriv (Radon–Nikodym theorem in Decomposition.RadonNikodym).

annotations.label: Weak convergence (in distribution)

anchor.snippet: converges weakly to the random variable

annotations.note: Convergence in distribution is TendstoInDistribution, the weak convergence of laws in the ProbabilityMeasure topology.

annotations.label: Convergence in probability

anchor.snippet: for every ε > 0

annotations.note: Convergence in probability is convergence in measure (TendstoInMeasure) with respect to a probability measure.

annotations.label: Strong (almost sure) convergence

anchor.snippet: Strong convergence is also known as almost sure convergence

annotations.note: Almost sure convergence is expressed as `∀ᵐ ω ∂μ, Tendsto ...` via the a.e. filter (ae), with no dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Hierarchy of convergence modes

anchor.snippet: strong convergence implies convergence in probability

annotations.note: In-probability⇒in-distribution is TendstoInMeasure.tendstoInDistribution, and a.s.⇒in-probability is tendstoInMeasure_of_tendsto_ae.

annotations.label: Law of large numbers

anchor.snippet: states that the sample average

annotations.note: The strong law of large numbers is strong_law_ae (sample averages converge a.s. to the mean).

annotations.label: Frequency converges to probability

anchor.snippet: It follows from the LLN that if an event of probability p is observed repeatedly

annotations.note: Relative frequency converging to p follows by applying the strong law to indicator random variables, but is not stated as a dedicated declaration.

annotations.label: Bernoulli average converges to p

anchor.snippet: are independent Bernoulli random variables taking values 1 with probability p

annotations.note: This is a special case of the strong law for iid Bernoulli variables, not formalized as its own example.

annotations.label: Central limit theorem

anchor.snippet: tends towards a normal distribution irrespective of the distribution

annotations.note: The 1-dimensional central limit theorem for iid variables is tendstoInDistribution_inv_sqrt_mul_sum_sub.

annotations.label: Berry–Esseen convergence rate

anchor.snippet: as illustrated in the Berry–Esseen theorem

annotations.note: No Berry–Esseen theorem (CLT convergence-rate bound) exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probability of entire sample space is 1

anchor.snippet: So, the probability of the entire sample space is 1

annotations.note: The total mass property μ(Ω) = 1 is exactly the defining condition of IsProbabilityMeasure.

annotations.label: Probability of null event is 0

anchor.snippet: the probability of the null event is 0

annotations.note: μ(∅) = 0 is measure_empty, a basic property of any measure.

annotations.label: Dirac delta function

anchor.snippet: is the Dirac delta function

annotations.note: The Dirac delta as a probability measure concentrated at a point is Measure.dirac.

annotations.label: σ-algebra

anchor.snippet: and a σ-algebra

annotations.note: A σ-algebra on a type is the data of a MeasurableSpace structure.

annotations.label: Borel σ-algebra

anchor.snippet: is the Borel σ-algebra on the set of real numbers

annotations.note: The Borel σ-algebra generated by the open sets is `borel`, with the BorelSpace typeclass on ℝ.

annotations.label: Counting measure

anchor.snippet: with respect to a counting measure

annotations.note: The counting measure is Measure.count.

annotations.label: Lebesgue measure

anchor.snippet: with respect to the Lebesgue measure

annotations.note: The Lebesgue measure on ℝ (and ℝⁿ) is the canonical `volume` instance.

annotations.label: Brownian motion

anchor.snippet: to study Brownian motion

annotations.note: Brownian motion / Wiener process is not yet formalized in Mathlib as a standalone construction.

annotations.label: Independent and identically distributed

anchor.snippet: of a sequence of independent and identically distributed random variables

annotations.note: Independent identically-distributed sequences are captured by ProbabilityTheory.iIndepFun together with ProbabilityTheory.IdentDistrib.

annotations.label: Expected value

anchor.snippet: converges towards their common expectation (expected value)

annotations.note: The expectation of a random variable is its Bochner integral against the underlying probability measure.

annotations.label: Variance

anchor.snippet: with finite variance

annotations.note: Variance Var(X) = E[(X − E[X])²] is ProbabilityTheory.variance.

annotations.label: Normal distribution

anchor.snippet: converges in distribution to a standard normal random variable

annotations.note: The (real) normal/Gaussian distribution is ProbabilityTheory.gaussianReal.
